coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional carbon dots for cell imaging and inhibition of human insulin fibrillation in the whole aggregation process
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bei-Bei
Wang, Yu-Ying
Zhang, Xiao-Yang
Xu, Zi-Qiang
Jiang, Peng
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Due to the favorable stability, water solubility and good biocompatibility, carbon dots have attracted much attention. Herein, a novel nitrogen-doping bifunctional carbon dots (N-BCDs) with ultra-highly quantum yield (QY abs = 70.4%) is prepared through microwave-assisted method. 50 μg/mL of N-BCDs emit intense fluorescence in HeLa and GES-1 cells with negligible cytotoxicity. In addition, effective inhibition of N-BCDs to human insulin (HI) fibrillation is observed even at 10:1 (mass ratio of HI: N-BCDs) by ThT fluorescence, CD assay and TEM. N-BCDs prevent HI from fibrillation with prolonged lag time and reduced fluorescent intensity at equilibrium, regardless of the addition time of N-BCDs (HI: N-BCDs = 1:1, mass ratio), which has been rarely reported before. Furthermore, the morphology of final HI fibrils is shorter and thinner in the presence of N-BCDs. Mechanism studies reveal that the enhanced hydrogen bond between HI monomers and N-BCDs inhibits nucleation during the lag stage (K a: 1.54 × 104 L·mol− 1, 298 K), while the accumulation of N-BCDs blocks the growth of profibrils in the elongation stage. To the best of our knowledge, it's the first time to observe the accumulation of N-BCDs around HI profibrils with TEM. Our study provides a new strategy for developing efficient nanoparticle inhibitors for protein fibrillation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Human insulin
Fibrillation inhibitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of N-BCDs on HI aggregation process
Photostability and cytotoxicity study of N-BCDs
Effect of N-BCDs on HI aggregation process
Mechanism of N-BCDs on HI fibrillation in the whole aggregation process

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of N-BCDs; (b) Size distribution of N-BCDs; (c) XRD spectra; (d) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra; (e) XPS spectra of N-BCDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-BCDs (red line) and PAS (blue line); (b) Fluorescent spectra of N-BCDs under different excitation wavelength; (c) Fluorescence decay curve of N-BCDs in aqueous solution; (d) FL intensity of N-BCDs under varies pH values; (e) FL intensity of N-BCDs in different ions strengths solution; (f) Cellular cytotoxicity of the N-BCDs for HeLa cells.

Fluorescence images of HeLa and GES-1 cells labeled with N-BCDs (excited by 405 nm laser), the scale bar is 30 μm: (a), (c), (e), (g) The images of untreated cells; (b), (d), (f), (h) The images of cells incubated with 50 μg/mL N-BCDs after 5 h.

(a), (b) The FL intensity of ThT at 485 nm as an indicator of Kinetics process of HI fibrillation (1 mg/mL), the final concentration is with 50 μM ThT in Tris-HCl buffer at pH 7.4; (c), (d) Morphologies of HI changing with time of HI only after incubation for 2 h and 3 h at 60 °C at pH 2.0; (e) The image of N-BCDs-HI protein system (HI:N-BCDs = 10:1) incubated after 2 h; (f) The image of N-BCDs-HI protein system (HI:N-BCDs = 10:5) after incubation 3 h.

(a) The FL intensity of ThT for HI fibrillation in the absence/presence of N-BCDs (HI:N-BCDs = 1:1); (b) CD spectra of N-BCDs-HI protein system (HI:N-BCDs = 1:1) at different time; (c) The TEM of HI only incubated after 5 h at 60 °C in pH 2.0; (d) The TEM image of N-BCDs-HI protein system (HI:N-BCDs = 1:1) incubated after 48 h under the same conditions.

(a) Lag time and final ThT FL intensity of N-BCDs-HI protein system (HI:N-BCDs = 1:1) incubated after 24 h at 60 °C in pH 2.0 by adding N-BCDs at different time points (0, 1, 2, 2.25 h); (b) TEM images of HI only without N-BCDs; (c) TEM images of N-BCDs added at 0 h before HI incubation; (d) High magnification image of the ellipsed regions of (c); (e) TEM images of N-BCDs added after HI incubation for 2.25 h; (f) High magnification image of (e).
Synthetic route of N-BCDs and the application for cell imaging and inhibition of HI fibrillation.
The quenching constants K sv, association constants K a and thermodynamic parameters of the interactions between N-BCDs and HI monomers.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional carbon dots for cell imaging and inhibition of human insulin fibrillation in the whole aggregation process

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (MOE)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the favorable stability, water solubility and good biocompatibility, carbon dots have attracted much attention. Herein, a novel nitrogen-doping bifunctional carbon dots (N-BCDs) with ultra-highly quantum yield (QY abs = 70.4%) is prepared through microwave-assisted method. 50 μg/mL of N-BCDs emit intense fluorescence in HeLa and GES-1 cells with negligible cytotoxicity. In addition, effective inhibition of N-BCDs to human insulin (HI) fibrillation is observed even at 10:1 (mass ratio of HI: N-BCDs) by ThT fluorescence, CD assay and TEM. N-BCDs prevent HI from fibrillation with prolonged lag time and reduced fluorescent intensity at equilibrium, regardless of the addition time of N-BCDs (HI: N-BCDs = 1:1, mass ratio), which has been rarely reported before. Furthermore, the morphology of final HI fibrils is shorter and thinner in the presence of N-BCDs. Mechanism studies reveal that the enhanced hydrogen bond between HI monomers and N-BCDs inhibits nucleation during the lag stage (K a: 1.54 × 104 L·mol− 1, 298 K), while the accumulation of N-BCDs blocks the growth of profibrils in the elongation stage. To the best of our knowledge, it's the first time to observe the accumulation of N-BCDs around HI profibrils with TEM. Our study provides a new strategy for developing efficient nanoparticle inhibitors for protein fibrillation.

ce:section.ce:para: More and more elderly people are suffering from type II diabetes and neurodegenerative disease, such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, which are all closely related to the highly ordered protein aggregation and amyloid fibrils formation [1,2]. Typically, the formation of amyloid fibrils is a self-assembly process, including lag stage, elongation stage, and saturation stage in which produce some toxic intermediate [3]. There is a growing interest in developing inhibitors to block the amyloid aggregation in order to reduce cytotoxicity, oxidative damage and cell death induced by intermediate oligomers or mature fibrils [3,4]. Traditional inhibitors include small molecules [5–7], peptide [8,9] and antibodies [3]. Due to the disadvantages of poor water solubility, high cytotoxicity and instability of traditional inhibitors, nanomaterials have been increasingly developed for inhibiting fibrillation with satisfactory results recently, including polymer particles, gold nanocluster, metal oxide nanoparticles, as well as quantum dots [10–12].

Compared with other nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have many advantages, such as excellent water solubility, extremely stability, good biocompatibility and relatively low-cost, making them attractive in biological and therapeutic applications [17–19]. Most importantly, CDs have the potential to easily pass through the blood-brain barrier and be removed by the organisms system due to their ultra-small particle sizes (<10 nm) [13,14]. Many attempts have been applied to synthesize CDs with bright fluorescence by heteroatom doping or surface passivation. Nitrogen was the most common doping element, which could form new surface state and alter the energy levels of CDs once introduced [20,21]. Moreover, nitrogen doping enriches the surface of CDs with nitrogen-containing groups that have great influence on its applications.

Unfortunately, many reports of CDs are focused on the applications in catalysis, sensing, bio-imaging and drug delivery [15,16]. Although there are a few fibrillation inhibitors based on CDs, the role of CDs played in the fibrillation process urgently needs to be further elucidated. Compared with other amyloidogenic systems, human insulin (HI) has higher solubility in aqueous solution and effective cost [5]. Furthermore, it is meaningful to study HI fibrillation process since HI is easily self-assembly into amyloid fibrils during the treatment of diabetes [22]. Thus, HI is selected as model protein to monitor the protein fibrillation process in this study. Recently, Li et al. reported that CDs synthesized by acid oxidation of carbon nanopowder, potently inhibited HI fibrillation and speculated the inhibition was likely due to the interaction between CDs and HI species in lag stage [12]. Our group has applied isothermal titration calorimetry technology to study the interaction between CDs and HI previously, indicating that the inhibition is mainly driven by electrostatic interactions between CDs and HI monomers. However, the inhibitory effect of CDs was limited once fibril seeds were introduced [4]. Keeping all these in mind, the development of highly effective inhibitors in the whole fibrillation of HI is urgently needed. What inspired us is that fluorine-functionalized quantum dots have been reported to display better inhibition against the aggregation of human islet amyloid polypeptide amyloid than that of bare graphene quantum dots by Yang et al. [11].

In this work, nitrogen-doping bifunctional carbon dots (N-BCDs) has been synthesized by microwave-assisted method and serves as both cell imaging agent and high-efficiency inhibitor against HI fibrillation (show in Scheme 1a). The inhibitory effect for HI fibrillation has been fully studied by Thioflavin T (ThT) fluorescence, circular dichroism (CD) assay and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Compare with other non-doping carbon dots, N-BCDs is preferable on the whole fibrillation since the internal structure of carbonization cores and the surface functional groups of carbon dots are greatly changed for nitrogen doping. To better understand the anti-fibrillation effect, the inhibitory mechanism has also been investigated using fluorescence titration experiments and TEM. Thus, our study provides a new strategy and direction for developing high-efficiency inhibitors for HI fibrillation based on carbon dots by heteroatom doping.

p-Aminosalicylic acid (PAS) was obtained from Macklin (Shanghai, China) and formamide was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HI was obtained from Yeasen Biological Technology Co. (Shanghai, China). Thioflavin T (ThT) was obtained from Sigma (USA). All other chemicals were used without further purification (analytical grade). Solutions were prepared with deionized water (Millipore, 18.0 MΩ cm).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired with JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (accelerating voltage of 200 kV). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was acquired on Rigaku Miniflex600. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was acquired using Thermo FTIR spectrophotometer. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on ESCALAB250Xi spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded on Cary Series UV–vis spectrophotometer and Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature. Absolute fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescent life were measured by FLS1000 Fluorescence Spectrometer.

N-BCDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted method. In brief, 0.5 g PAS was dissolved into 5 mL formamide and the mixture was transferred into 20 mL microwave tube, followed by heating at 180 °C for 30 min. After the system cooled to room temperature naturally, the black solution diluted with deionized water (V reaction:V H2O = 1:1) and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to remove the precipitates. Then the supernatant was dialyzed against deionized water for two days with water replacement three times per day (MWCO: 1000 Da). Finally, the solution in dialysis bag was filtered through 0.22 μm polyethersulfone (PES) water phase membrane and lyophilized to obtain the powder product.

HeLa and GES-1 cells were gifts from Prof. Xiang Zhou's group at College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University. Cells were cultured in DMEM (Dulbecco modified Eagle medium) supplementing with 10% FBS (fetal bovine serum) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. The cytotoxicity of N-BCDs was assessed by standard MTT ((3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5diphenyltetrazolium bromide)) assay. Cells adhered onto 96-well plates at 37 °C in 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h in advance. After that, cells continued to culture with fresh medium containing different concentrations of N-BCDs for a certain time (24 h or 48 h). Then MTT (5 mg/mL, 20 μL) was added into each well for another 4 h. Then 150 μL of DMSO were added into each well to dissolve the purple crystal after removing the culture medium. The absorption at 490 nm was recorded by Elx800 Micropalte reader (BioTek, USA). For cellular imaging, cells were re-incubated with 50 μg/mL N-BCDs solution for 5 h after the incubation to adhere in culture dishes for 24 h. Then the cells were washed with PBS for three times and visualized by confocal fluorescence imaging (excited: 405 nm, Perkin Elmer Ultra VIEW VoX).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of N-BCDs on HI aggregation process

ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, HI was dissolved in HFP (1,1,1,3,3,3-hexafluoroisopropanol), stirring at 120 rpm for 12 h to obtain HI monomers. Then solution was dried with nitrogen and re-dissolved in 100 mM HCl-NaCl (pH 2.0) with the final concentration of 1 mg/mL for further use. To monitor the HI fibrillation process, 1 mL of HI protein (1 mg/mL) solutions containing different concentration of N-BCDs were incubated at 60 °C in pH 2.0. Then 40 μL of the samples was taken out from the mixture at different time interval. The FL intensity of ThT (thioflavin T, 20 μM) in 2 mL solution containing 20 μL of different time sample at 485 nm was recorded on LS-55 (excited: 450 nm, Perkin Elmer).

20 μL of different time point sample was diluted with 200 μL PBS (pH 7.0). Then the CD spectra were acquired by circular dichroism photomultiplier (Applied Photophysics Limited, UK) ranging from 260 to 200 nm at 25 °C. Each spectrum was the average of three scans.

Fluorescence analysis was performed on LS-55 fluorophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) equipped with circulating bath to maintain the temperature. Titrations were performed by the micro-syringe. After each addition of N-BCDs, the emission spectra of HI were measured between 280 nm–350 nm excited at 280 nm (excitation slit: 5 nm, emission slit: 10 nm). The concentration of HI was kept at 10 μM (HCl-NaCl 2.0). 1 mg/mL of N-BCDs were injected into 2 mL of HI solution by 4 μL each time with continuous stirring. Each spectrum was the average of three scans.

N-BCDs have been synthesized by microwave-assisted method which could shorten reaction time effectively and provide high pressure at the same time [23,24]. The N-BCDs with uniform size can be achieved by the continuous, rapid and homogeneous heating during the microwave-assisted strategy [11]. Furthermore, the highly quantum yield and the uniformity of surface state of N-BCDs can be ensured by single precursor as carbon source and nitrogen source with formamide solvent.

Data indicated that the emission wavelength and intensity of N-BCDs were not significantly influenced by the ratio of precursor and solvent, but were effectively tuned by reaction temperature and reaction time (fixed the ratio of PAS and formamide as 1:10 (m/v), Fig. S1). The FL of the reaction solution reached maximum value at 180 °C (Fig. S1b). It should be noticed that although the intense FL intensity was observed at the reaction time of 10 min, only at the reaction time of 30 min that obvious nanoparticles were occurred in TEM images (Figs. 1a, S2). These results suggested that the formation process of N-BCDs originated from PAS is similar to that of small molecular like critic acid, L-cysteine et al., in which decomposition, self-polymerization, carbonization, and surface passivation were involved [25,26]. Therefore, the optimal reaction conditions are selected as the ratio of PAS: formamide = 1:10 (m/v), 180 °C, 30 min and the obtained product are used for the following experiments at this condition.

As shown in Fig. 1a, the obtained N-BCDs are almost spherical nanoparticles with the average size of 2.54 ± 0.48 nm (Fig. 1b). Well-resolved lattice fringe spacing about 0.21 nm is observed in high-resolution TEM image, corresponding to the d-spacing (100) planes of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1a, inset) [27]. The existence of the fringe spacing also confirms the formation of sp2 graphitic carbon inner core [28]. XRD spectrum displays a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 25°, corresponding to the existence of (002) lattice spacing of graphic carbon (Fig. 1c) [18].

The surface functional groups and element states were studied by FTIR spectra and XPS spectra. The broad bands at 3460–3342 cm− 1 and 3274–3171 cm− 1 in FTIR correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, suggesting the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups (Fig. 1d). The vibrational absorption peaks at 1686 and 1605 cm− 1 are the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, respectively [14]. The C-O-C and CN stretching vibrations were located at 1167 and 1252 cm− 1. Specially, characteristic peaks at 1686 and 1383 cm− 1 are assigned to the vibrations of amide's CO and CN bond, indicating the presence of amide bond [13]. The XPS spectra of N-BCDs present three obvious peaks at 284.5 eV, 400.2 eV and 530.8 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s (Fig. 1e). The high resolution spectrum of C1s is fitted into four peaks at 284.7 eV (C=C), 285.5 eV (C-C/C-N), 286.4 eV (C-OH) and 288.3 eV (COOH) (Fig. S3a) [19,29]. These indicate that N-BCDs are enriched with hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups (Table S1). The high resolution spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s confirmed the existence of CC, CN, CO, C-C/C-N, C-O-C and C-OH bond of N-BCDs, consistent with the structure results of FTIR (Fig. S3). All these results indicate the introduction of amino groups to the surface of N-BCDs and the successful doping of nitrogen atoms into carbonization cores.

There are two peaks at 242 nm and 306 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-BCDs, corresponding to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domain [13,30] and surface states which trap the excited energy leading to bright fluorescence [12,31] (Fig. 2a, red line). According to previous reports, such absorption spectrum can be ascribed to the complicated structures and random energy levels of the N-BCDs [12]. Transparent color solution of N-BCDs in water emits bright fluorescence under ultraviolet excitation (λ ex = 365 nm) (inset of Fig. 2b). Interestingly, the maximum emission wavelength of N-BCDs keeps constant at 463 nm when the excitation wavelengths are ranging from 340 nm to 420 nm, namely, N-BCDs exhibits an excitation-independent emission feature (Fig. 2b). This property could be illustrated with the narrow size distribution and uniform surface structure of N-BCDs, consistent with previous reported CDs [32,33]. The optical excitation and emission wavelength of N-BCDs are 395 nm and 463 nm with absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY abs ) of 70.4% (Fig. S4). The use of single precursor leads to the uniformity of surface states, resulting in highly QY of N-BCDs. The fluorescence decay curve of N-BCDs in aqueous solution fits pretty well with single exponential decay curve and the average fluorescent lifetime is 3.68 ns (χ 2 = 1.149), indicating the surface defect emission is dominated in the N-BCDs solution (Fig. 2c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability and cytotoxicity study of N-BCDs
Effect of N-BCDs on HI aggregation process
ce:section.ce:para:
The sigmoid kinetics growth curve is appeared during HI aggregation process, consisting of lag stage, elongation stage, and saturation stage according to previous reports [34]. The stages could be monitored and easily distinguished by the FL change of ThT. ThT is a general dye that specially binds between β-sheet structures of amyloid fibrils, resulting in an intense enhancement of FL intensity [35–37]. The variation FL intensity could be fitted with Eq. (1) below and the lag time is calculated using Eq. (2), where F 0 is the initial fluorescence intensity, F max is the FL intensity at equilibrium, k is the elongation rate, t lag is the time of lag stage and t 1/2 is the time required to reach the half of F max [34,37].
(1)
F =
F 0
+
F max
1 +
e
− k
t −
t
1 / 2
(2)
t lag
=
t
1 / 2
−
2 k

As shown in Fig. 4a (black line), HI monomers (1.0 mg/mL) in pH 2.0 HCl-NaCl solution undergoes about 2.3 h of lag stage, followed by 1 h of elongation stage, and finally reaches saturation stage after 4 h of incubation at 60 °C. However, the lag stage of HI aggregation was prolonged to 2.5 h in the presence of N-BCDs (HI: N-BCDs = 10:3, mass ratio) while it was almost not influenced by the co-incubation with PAS (HI: PAS = 10:3, the precursor used) under the same conditions (Fig. 4a). The inhibitory effect of N-BCDs on HI fibrillation displayed a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 4b) and the relevant parameters in the process were shown in Table S2 (fixed HI at 1.0 mg/mL, HI: N-BCDs = 10:1, 10:3, 10:5, 10:7). The higher concentration of N-BCDs was, the longer lag stage acquired (Table S2 and Fig. 4b). Moreover, the elongation rate k was reduced and inversely correlated with the concentration of N-BCDs except for the group of HI: N-BCDs = 10:7. These results clearly indicate that the N-BCDs could be used as an inhibitor for the HI fibrillation.

The secondary conformational changes of HI in the fibrillation were studied by CD spectrum. There are two negative peaks centered at 208 and 222 nm of HI in HCl-NaCl (pH 2.0) solution, which were attributed to the characteristic of α-helixes (Fig. S6a, b). In the HI only group, the two peaks gradually disappeared and a new broad peak at 219 nm formed after incubating for 3 h at 60 °C for β-sheet of HI by partial or complete de-folding, respectively. HI completely unfolded and the negative peak wavelength shifted to 220 nm after 6 h. However, the conformation of HI remained almost unchanged after 2.5 h and turned to β-sheet at 4 h in the presence of N-BCDs (HI:N-BCDs = 10:3) (Fig. S6c, d). With the concentration of N-BCDs increased, longer incubation time were required to change the α-helix structure of HI to β-sheet, in agreement with the ThT assay (Fig. S6e–h). These indicate that N-BCDs stabilized α-helix and unordered structures of HI. To visually observe and monitor the formation of these fibrils, TEM images of incubation solutions withdrawn at different times were obtained. The morphology of HI without N-BCDs was observed in oligomers and a small amount of protofibrils started to form after the incubation at 60 °C for 2 h (Fig. 4c). And large numbers of fibrils was appeared at 3 h (Fig. 4d). However, HI remained in monomers and oligomers for 2 h without the occurrence of protofibrils when incubated with N-BCDs at mass ratio of 10:1 (Fig. 4e). There was little protofibrils at mass ratio of 10:5 for 3 h. It is clearly that nucleation process was slowed down with the addition of N-BCDs.

Interestingly, the presence of N-BCDs could also affect the morphology of the final fibrils. With increasing the ratio of N-BCDs to HI to 1:1, the lag stage prolonged to 5.45 h (Fig. 5a). And the native conformation of HI remained α-helix structure even after 6 h while the HI only group has entirely converted to β-sheets (Fig. 5b). Only shorter and thinner fibrils were observed at 12 h (HI: N-BCDs = 1:1) while mature longer and thicker fibrils of HI alone was formed at 60 °C for 5 h in TEM images (Figs. S7, 5c). And these fibrils remained thin after 48 h (HI: N-BCDs = 1:1) (Fig. 5d). Taken these data together, we conclude that the presence of N-BCDs not only delays the lag stage and slow down the elongation rate, but also make the final fibrils shorter and thinner.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of N-BCDs on HI fibrillation in the whole aggregation process
ce:section.ce:para:
Firstly, the interaction between N-BCDs and HI monomers were investigated by fluorescence titration experiments at different temperatures (Fig. S8). The FL intensity of HI monomers was gradually quenched with the addition of N-BCDs while the emission peaks remain constant (Fig. S8a), indicating N-BCDs doesn't affect the micro-environment of aromatic amino acids residue (Tyrosine and Phenylalanine that contribute to the fluorescence of HI protein). And this also indicates the binding site of N-BCDs is not related to Tyr (Tyrosine) and Phe (Phenylalanine) residue. Specially, the fragment of Phe-Phe-Tyr in B-chain of HI easily forms π-π stacking interaction with small aromatic molecules [38], so we conclude that there is no π-π stacking interaction between N-BCDs and HI protein. The fluorescence change of HI monomers is fitted by the classical Stern-Volmer equation (SV, Eq. (3)) (Fig. S8b). F 0 is the initial FL intensity of HI monomers, F is the FL intensity of HI monomers after each addition of N-BCDs, [C] is the concentration of N-BCDs. The quenching constant K SV decreased as the temperature raised, indicating static quenching is dominated, namely complex are formed between N-BCDs and HI monomer rather than random collisions interaction in the process (Table 1 ). K a, the effective quenching constant as well as binding constant, is also decreased with the increasing of temperature (Fig. S8c), confirming static quenching is involved (modified SV equation, Eq. (4)). Here, f a is the fraction of the fluorescent group and quenching agent [39].
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
C
(4)
F 0
F 0
− F
=
1
f a
1
K a
+
1
f a

According to previous reports, the main types of binding forces between nanoparticles and biomacromolecules can be approximately determined by the thermodynamic parameters of the interaction process [39–41]. The change of reaction enthalpy is regarded as constant in small range of temperature, so van't Hoff equation (Eq. (5)) can be applied to caculate the enthalpy change (∆H), the entropy change (∆S) (Fig. S8d). And the free energy change (∆G) could be calculated accordingly (Eq. (6)). Ross's theory indicates that typical hydrophobic forces is dominated when ∆H > 0, ∆S > 0 while hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces are dominated when ∆H < 0, ∆S < 0 [42].
(5)
ln
K a
= −
∆ H
RT
+
∆ S
R
(6)
∆ G = − RT
ln
K a

In our case, the interaction between N-BCDs and HI monomers are mainly hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces in enthalpy-driven way (∆H < 0, ∆S < 0) (shown in Fig. S8 and Table 1). As we know, the exposure of hydrophobic amino acid residues and unfolding of secondary structures in HI is crucial to the fibrillation process [9]. The hydrogen bonds between N-BCDs and HI containing polar groups have been enhanced due to the successful doping of nitrogen in carbon core which increases the polarity of N-BCDs and the positive charge over the aromatic ring [11]. The enhanced hydrogen bonds decrease the exposure of hydrophobic core in native HI protein resulting in the extension of lag time in the lag stage.

To further study the inhibition mechanism of N-BCDs in elongation stage, the kinetic curves of HI aggregation were monitored in which N-BCDs were added at different time points (HI:N-BCDs = 1:1, t = 0, 1, 2, 2.25 h) (Fig. S9). Results suggest that the earlier N-BCDs added, the longer lag stage occurred (black bars, Figs. 6a and S9). But the final ThT fluorescence intensity was 4.2-fold lower than that of HI only group even N-BCDs were added at the beginning of elongation stage (t = 2.25 h, Fig. S9d). These results indicate that N-BCDs possess the ability to block the oligomers aggregation and protofibrils growth although the nucleation has completed, which has rarely been reported (Fig. S9d). This phenomenon has also been observed by TEM. The mature HI fibrils are all shorter and thinner in the presence of N-BCDs than that of HI only group, regardless of the addition time (Fig. 6c, e). Interestingly, large amount of N-BCDs are observed around the fibrils. The size distribution of N-BCDs is 2.75 ± 0.39 nm which is similar with 2.54 ± 0.48 nm (Fig. 6f). These results confirm that the interaction between N-BCDs and oligomers prevents the aggregation of oligomers/protofibrils for mature fibrils. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time to observe the accumulation of N-BCDs on HI fibrils with TEM. Although there is not sufficient evidence to elucidate the specific site of interaction between nitrogen-containing groups of N-BCDs and oligomers, Khan et al. have reported that the small molecule with amino groups could form strong hydrogen bond with bovine insulin based on molecular docking [43].

Herein, we speculate that amino groups may significantly reduce the intra- and inter-chain interactions of HI oligomers. And HI prefers to interact with the N-BCDs rather than other oligomers in the elongation stage, resulting in the increase of thermal stability and blockage of self-aggregation of oligomers in the fibrillation process. These results also indicate that the surface functional groups of CDs have great effects on HI inhibition.

In summary, novel N-BCDs has been synthesized by microwave-assisted method. As expected, N-BCDs emit intense fluorescence both in HeLa and GES-1 cells with negligible cytotoxicity. In addition, the N-BCDs could serve as an effective inhibitor for HI fibrillation in the whole process. And the existence of N-BCDs also makes the final fibrils shorter and thinner than that of HI only group. Mechanism studies suggest that the enhanced hydrogen bond interactions between N-BCDs and HI monomers/oligomers prolong the lag time while the accumulation of N-BCDs block the self-aggregation of protofibrils in the elongation stage. Our results provide a new strategy for developing highly effective nanomaterial inhibitors of protein fibrillation by heteroatom doping.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Bei-Bei Wang:Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Writing-original draft.Yu-Ying Wang:Investigation, Methodology.Xiao-Yang Zhang:Investigation, Methodology.Zi-Qiang Xu:Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Writing - review & editing.Peng Jiang:Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Writing - review & editing.Feng-Lei Jiang:Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Writing - review & editing.Yi Liu:Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21873075, 21603067), National Special Subject of Infectious Diseases (2018ZX10301405), Guangxi Science and Technology Project (GuiKeAD17195081) and Bagui Scholar Program of Guangxi Province (2016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2019.12.267.
